title: What is Naive Bayes?
concept: Naive Bayes is a fast probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' Theorem, commonly used as a strong baseline for text classification.

exercises.prompt: What mathematical concept is Naive Bayes based on?
exercises.explain: Bayes' Theorem is a mathematical formula for calculating probabilities based on prior knowledge, which is the foundation of the Naive Bayes classifier.

cards.title: Sorting at Light Speed
cards.body: How do computers instantly sort millions of emails into 'Spam' and 'Inbox' folders without breaking a sweat? They use a classic, super-fast probability trick called Naive Bayes!

cards.title: Defining Naive Bayes
cards.body: Naive Bayes is a simple machine learning ruleset that calculates probabilities. It guesses the category of a piece of text (like an email being 'Spam' or 'Inbox') based on the math of Bayes' Theorem. It is famous for being incredibly fast and a great starting point for text projects.

cards.title: The Mail Sorter
cards.body: Imagine you are sorting incoming mail. Instead of reading every single word deeply, you just look at key words. If you see 'winner', 'cash', and 'free', you immediately think 'Spam!'. You guess the category based on how often those words appear in spam versus real mail, treating each word like an independent clue.

cards.title: Spam Filtering
cards.body: When you build a spam filter, Naive Bayes calculates the probability that an email is spam given the words it contains. If the word 'viagra' has a 99% chance of being in spam, and 'meeting' has a 99% chance of being in inbox, it multiplies these simple probabilities to make a quick final guess.

cards.title: Naive Doesn't Mean Weak
cards.body: Many people think a 'naive' algorithm must be weak or useless. In reality, even though its assumptions are simple, Naive Bayes is incredibly powerful and often beats much more complex systems at text classification tasks.

cards.title: Word Independence
cards.body: Let's compare how Naive Bayes treats words compared to how humans read them.

left.text: Treats every word as a separate, independent clue. (e.g., 'very' and 'good' are unrelated)

right.text: Understands word order and context. (e.g., 'not good' means the opposite of 'good')

exercises.prompt: What is a major advantage of using Naive Bayes?

options.text: It is fast and easy to train.
It requires massive amounts of supercomputer power.
It understands the deep emotional meaning of sentences.
It takes days to process a single email.

exercises.explain: Naive Bayes is highly celebrated for being very fast to train and run, making it an excellent baseline model.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Naive Bayes is commonly used as a strong baseline model for text classification.
exercises.explain: Naive Bayes is widely used as a benchmark or 'baseline' because it is quick to set up and hard to beat for basic text tasks.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete how Naive Bayes makes its decisions.
exercises.sentence: Naive Bayes uses rules of probability to guess the category of a text.
exercises.explain: Naive Bayes calculates the probability of each category based on the words it sees, choosing the one with the highest likelihood.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes how Naive Bayes actually works?

options.text: It calculates the probability of a category based on the frequency of words.
It uses quantum mechanics to imagine what the text is thinking.
It builds deep semantic neural paths to feel the vibe of the text.

exercises.explain: Option B is created by an AI because it mentions 'imagining' and 'quantum mechanics,' which are irrelevant to this simple probability model. Option C is also AI-generated as it mentions 'deep semantic neural paths.' Option A is the correct human explanation.

exercises.prompt: What is the main 'naive' assumption that Naive Bayes makes when classifying text?

options.text: It assumes all words depend on the order they are written in.
It calculates the exact physical weight of the text.
It assumes every word is completely independent of the other words.
It reads the writer's mind using advanced neural networks.

exercises.explain: Naive Bayes makes a 'naive' assumption that all features (like words in a text) are completely independent of each other. Even though this assumption is rarely true in real life, the algorithm still works surprisingly well and fast!
